title: Acute Hemolytic Transfusion Reaction at 30 Minutes Into Second Unit PRBC in 67-Year-Old Man Post-Operative Day 1 After Open AAA Repair: ABO Incompatibility from Clerical Mismatch on Cross-Match Sample Collected Pre-Room-Transfer, Immediate STOP + Maintain IV with Separate Normal Saline Line, DIC + AKI Surveillance, Blood Bank Workup, Sentinel Event Root Cause Analysis, and Just Culture System Response
subtitle: ACUTE HEMOLYTIC TRANSFUSION REACTION (AHTR) = LIFE-THREATENING REACTION typically occurs within MINUTES to 1 HOUR of starting incompatible blood + caused most often by ABO incompatibility from CLERICAL ERROR (wrong patient sample + wrong patient label + wrong patient at bedside) — RARELY immune incompatibility despite proper cross-match; CLINICAL FEATURES = FEVER + CHILLS + BACK/FLANK PAIN + tachycardia + hypotension + RED/DARK URINE (hemoglobinuria from intravascular hemolysis) + dyspnea + chest pain + nausea/vomiting + sense of impending doom + can progress to SHOCK + DIC + AKI + DEATH; PATHOPHYSIOLOGY = recipient antibodies bind donor RBC ABO antigens → COMPLEMENT activation → intravascular hemolysis → release of free hemoglobin + cytokines + activation of coagulation cascade → DIC + acute kidney injury from heme pigment nephrotoxicity + tubular obstruction + shock; IMMEDIATE NURSING RESPONSE: (1) STOP TRANSFUSION IMMEDIATELY at first sign of reaction (do NOT wait for severity); (2) MAINTAIN IV ACCESS with NS via SEPARATE TUBING (NOT same line — disconnect transfusion tubing + new tubing prevents continued exposure); (3) NOTIFY PROVIDER + BLOOD BANK STAT; (4) VITAL SIGNS q5-15 min; (5) MONITOR for shock + DIC + AKI; (6) MAINTAIN IV FLUIDS to support BP + URINE OUTPUT >100 mL/hr to prevent heme-pigment nephrotoxicity + tubular obstruction; (7) COLLECT post-transfusion samples — blood (for repeat ABO/Rh + cross-match + DAT/Coombs + free hemoglobin + LDH + haptoglobin + bilirubin + DIC panel) + urine (for hemoglobin + UA) + RETURN remaining blood unit + tubing + filter to blood bank; (8) DOCUMENT meticulously — exact time of reaction onset + symptoms + interventions + responses + provider notification + blood bank workup; (9) SUPPORT patient + family + emotional response; (10) PARTICIPATE in sentinel event root cause analysis. DIFFERENTIAL of transfusion reactions: (1) AHTR (ABO incompatibility) — minutes-1 hr; FEVER + CHILLS + BACK/FLANK PAIN + hypotension + hemoglobinuria; STOP + supportive; (2) FEBRILE NON-HEMOLYTIC (FNHR — cytokine release) — fever 1-6°F above baseline + chills without hemolysis; mild + supportive + acetaminophen; can sometimes restart slowly OR stop based on severity; leukoreduced blood reduces incidence; (3) ALLERGIC mild = urticaria + pruritus → STOP + diphenhydramine + may restart slowly if mild + resolves; SEVERE anaphylaxis (rare; classic in IgA-deficient patients with anti-IgA antibodies) → STOP + epinephrine 0.3-0.5 mg IM + IV fluids + airway + supportive + washed cells future; (4) TRALI (Transfusion-Related Acute Lung Injury) — within 6 hr (typically 1-2 hr); HYPOXIA + bilateral pulmonary edema NONCARDIOGENIC + fever + tachypnea + tachycardia; STOP + supportive + ventilatory support + most common cause TRANSFUSION-RELATED MORTALITY per FDA; donor antibodies cause neutrophil sequestration in lungs; (5) TACO (Transfusion-Associated Circulatory Overload) — within 6 hr; FLUID OVERLOAD + dyspnea + HTN + JVD + tachycardia + pulmonary edema CARDIOGENIC + bilateral infiltrates; STOP + diuresis + slow rate + reassess + reduce volume + supplemental O2; common in elderly + heart failure + renal failure; (6) BACTERIAL CONTAMINATION (sepsis from contaminated blood) — typically within hours; FEVER + CHILLS + HYPOTENSION + SHOCK + rigors + ~30 min-2 hr; STOP + blood cultures + broad-spectrum antibiotics + supportive + GRAM STAIN of blood unit + report; PLATELETS more often contaminated than RBC (stored at room temp); (7) DELAYED HEMOLYTIC (3-14 days) — milder + Hgb drop + jaundice + positive DAT + antibody screening; (8) GVHD (Graft-vs-Host Disease) — rare; transfusion of immunocompetent donor lymphocytes to immunocompromised host; fever + rash + diarrhea + pancytopenia; PREVENTION via IRRADIATED blood products in at-risk patients (BMT + chemo + congenital immunodeficiency); (9) IRON OVERLOAD in chronic transfusion patients; (10) IATROGENIC (volume overload + electrolytes — citrate hypocalcemia + hyperkalemia). PREVENTION = 2-PATIENT IDENTIFIER CHECK (name + DOB OR MRN) at EVERY step (sample collection + cross-match label + bedside + administration) + 2-RN VERIFICATION at bedside (read aloud label + bag + patient identifiers + cross-match form + verify ABO/Rh + expiration + indication + appearance + signature) + barcode scanning + LEUKOREDUCED + IRRADIATED + filtered products + slow start (15-30 min initial rate) + VITAL SIGNS pre-transfusion + 15 min + q30-60 min + post-transfusion + ongoing monitoring + RN at bedside during initial 15 min + DOCUMENT meticulously

labs.v: 67 y/o male G. O'Brien admitted POD 1 after ELECTIVE OPEN ABDOMINAL AORTIC ANEURYSM (AAA) REPAIR yesterday for 5.8 cm infrarenal AAA (asymptomatic + identified on routine screening). PMH: CAD (CABG 4 yr ago), HTN, hyperlipidemia, type 2 diabetes (controlled HbA1c 7.0), former smoker quit × 3 yr (60 pack-years), benign prostatic hyperplasia, mild chronic kidney disease (baseline Cr 1.2 — Stage 3a). SOCIAL: married 42 years to wife (64, retired schoolteacher — at bedside throughout); 2 adult children (lives nearby + supportive); retired carpenter × 5 years; engaged in community + church + golf. SURGICAL HISTORY: AAA repair yesterday (open transabdominal approach, 4-hr operative time, 1.2 L estimated blood loss, intraoperative cell saver, 2 units PRBC given intraoperatively, no complications, extubated in OR, transferred to PACU then to surgical floor); also CABG 4 yr ago, cholecystectomy 12 yr ago, appendectomy childhood. ALLERGIES: iodine contrast (mild rash + not anaphylactic — pre-medicated for studies as needed); shellfish (mild GI upset); NKDA medications. PREOP labs: Hgb 13.4, Hct 39, platelets 220, INR 1.0, PT/PTT normal, BMP normal, type & screen done preoperatively (O positive). POST-OP day 0: 2 units PRBC given intraoperatively for blood loss; arrived to PACU stable + transferred to floor Bed 612 (semi-private) at 1800. POD 1 (yesterday) morning 0830: Hgb 8.0 (recovered with cell saver + 2 intraop units); patient stable + pain controlled + ambulating; cross-match sample drawn at 0830 for POTENTIAL future transfusion (anticipating need for 2 more units given large surgery + lower preop Hgb). POD 1 (yesterday) afternoon 1530: ROOM TRANSFER from Bed 612 → Bed 414 due to facility bed reorganization (semi-private bed needed for incoming patient); transfer documented; cross-match sample from 0830 still in lab + correctly attributed to G. O'Brien by name + DOB + MRN. POD 1 evening: Hgb 7.5 (continuing decline); pain controlled; UOP adequate. POD 2 (today) morning 0700: Hgb 7.2 (further decline below transfusion threshold for post-cardiac/post-vascular surgery); order placed for 2 units PRBC. POD 2 (today) 0830: 2nd cross-match sample drawn 'to confirm fresh sample' per institutional protocol (some institutions require fresh sample within 72 hr of transfusion + this institution requires within 24 hr) — HERE IS WHERE THE ERROR OCCURRED: bedside RN drawing the sample did NOT perform 2-patient identifier check + used the BED LOCATION label alone for sample collection + labeled tube based on bed label (Bed 414 = G. O'Brien per system) — HOWEVER the sample was actually drawn from the WRONG patient in the ADJACENT bed (a different patient who had moved into Bed 612 after G. O'Brien left yesterday; this patient was being prepped for procedures + had similar IV access + the RN was distracted + grabbed sample from wrong bedside) — the bedside RN should have verified patient identity with TWO IDENTIFIERS (name + DOB) at the patient's wristband BEFORE drawing the sample; the lab cross-matched this WRONG sample + dispensed 2 units against this wrong sample's blood type + sent to floor for G. O'Brien (correctly per chart attribution); thus the 2nd unit was actually cross-matched against another patient's blood + was ABO INCOMPATIBLE with G. O'Brien who is O+ (the actual donor unit was apparently B+ or AB+ — incompatible with O+ recipient who has anti-A + anti-B antibodies). POD 2 1300-1500: UNIT 1 transfused (was the correctly cross-matched leftover from previous sample) UNEVENTFULLY — patient tolerated well, Hgb up to 8.2 post-Unit 1. POD 2 1500-1530: UNIT 2 started — at 30 min into transfusion patient develops AHTR symptoms (fever 39 + chills + back/flank pain + tachycardia + hypotension + dark urine). RN IMMEDIATELY recognizes + STOPS transfusion + maintains IV access via separate tubing + escalates + supportive care + investigation. CROSS-MATCH MISMATCH discovered + sentinel event confirmed + root cause analysis activated.
labs.r: POSTOPERATIVE TRANSFUSION + acute transfusion reaction in elderly patient with vascular disease. The CRITICAL ERROR was the cross-match sample drawn from the WRONG patient + labeled based on bed location alone WITHOUT 2-patient identifier check — a CLERICAL ERROR that bypassed multiple safety checks. RECOGNITION + IMMEDIATE STOP + supportive care + escalation + sentinel event analysis. RN role: recognize transfusion reaction + initial stabilization + investigation participation + system improvement.

labs.v: TRANSFUSION SAFETY procedures per AABB (American Association of Blood Banks) + Joint Commission + CMS standards: (1) PRE-TRANSFUSION SAMPLE COLLECTION: BEDSIDE 2-PATIENT IDENTIFIER CHECK using TWO INDEPENDENT IDENTIFIERS — NAME (full name including spelling) + DATE OF BIRTH + MEDICAL RECORD NUMBER (MRN) — verify against PATIENT WRISTBAND + PATIENT VERBAL CONFIRMATION (when alert) + ORDER + LAB FORM; this is BEFORE drawing sample; labels printed from system + applied to sample at the bedside BY THE PERSON DRAWING THE SAMPLE (not later); LABELS placed on sample in patient's presence; date + time + initials of collector; CHAIN OF CUSTODY maintained; bed location alone IS NOT a sufficient identifier (rooms change; patients move; beds get reassigned); (2) LAB CROSS-MATCH: ABO + Rh typing + antibody screen + cross-match donor unit against recipient serum; sample valid for limited time (typically 72 hr in non-transfused patients + 24-72 hr in transfused/pregnant patients due to potential new antibody formation); each institutional policy specifies; (3) DISPENSE FROM BLOOD BANK: 2-PERSON CHECK at blood bank — patient identifiers + unit number + ABO/Rh + cross-match results + expiration + integrity inspection; (4) TRANSPORT to floor — temperature-controlled + minimal time + chain of custody maintained; (5) BEDSIDE VERIFICATION BEFORE TRANSFUSION: 2-RN INDEPENDENT VERIFICATION (sometimes RN + LPN or RN + MD per institutional policy + state regulations; 2 licensed personnel + 1 RN minimum); BOTH read aloud + verify: PATIENT IDENTIFIERS (name + DOB + MRN) at WRISTBAND + verbal confirmation + UNIT LABEL identifiers (matching), UNIT NUMBER + ABO/Rh (matching recipient — for O+ recipient = O+ or O-; for compatibility table), EXPIRATION date + time + integrity (no clots + no discoloration + no leaks), CROSS-MATCH FORM signature + matching, INDICATION + ORDER; barcode scanning if institution has; document signatures; (6) PRE-TRANSFUSION VITAL SIGNS: HR + BP + RR + SpO2 + temp documented within 30 min of start; (7) SLOW START first 15-30 min at reduced rate (50-75 mL/hr for adult PRBC) + RN AT BEDSIDE FOR FIRST 15 MIN watching for reaction; (8) VITAL SIGNS during transfusion: at 15 min + every 30-60 min thereafter + post-transfusion + 1-hr post; (9) MONITOR for transfusion reaction throughout; (10) DOCUMENTATION of all checks + signatures + vitals + tolerance + any events. ABO COMPATIBILITY TABLE for PRBC: TYPE O = UNIVERSAL RECIPIENT for plasma (can receive any type) + UNIVERSAL DONOR for RBC (O can be given to A, B, AB, O); TYPE A receives O or A; TYPE B receives O or B; TYPE AB receives O, A, B, AB = universal recipient for RBC; Rh negative receives Rh negative or positive; Rh positive can receive either. ABO INCOMPATIBILITY = recipient has antibodies against donor RBC antigens (e.g., type O has anti-A + anti-B; receiving A or B causes hemolysis) = AHTR. Plasma: TYPE AB plasma = universal donor for plasma (no antibodies); TYPE O plasma = universal recipient for plasma (no antigens; can only receive O plasma — has anti-A + anti-B antibodies). LEUKOREDUCED blood (most modern PRBC) = white cells removed during processing — reduces febrile non-hemolytic reactions + CMV transmission + alloimmunization; standard most institutions. IRRADIATED blood for at-risk patients (BMT + chemotherapy + congenital immunodeficiency + intrauterine + neonatal exchange + Hodgkin lymphoma) — prevents GVHD; standard for at-risk populations. WASHED RBC for IgA-deficient or history of severe allergic reactions — removes plasma. CMV-NEGATIVE blood for selected at-risk populations (CMV-negative organ transplant + pregnant + immunocompromised — leukoreduction often sufficient).
labs.r: TRANSFUSION SAFETY PROCEDURES = multi-layered system with 2-PATIENT IDENTIFIER CHECK + 2-RN VERIFICATION + barcode scanning + ABO compatibility + integrity check + slow start + vital signs + monitoring + documentation. WRONG-PATIENT identification at ANY step (sample, cross-match, dispense, bedside) = AHTR risk. Standard practice + Joint Commission required. RN role: 2-patient identifier check + 2-RN verification + bedside monitoring + immediate recognition + immediate stop.

labs.v: ACUTE HEMOLYTIC TRANSFUSION REACTION (AHTR) clinical features: typically WITHIN MINUTES TO 1 HOUR of starting incompatible blood; classic symptoms: (1) FEVER + CHILLS (rigors); (2) BACK + FLANK PAIN (lumbar pain — distinctive; reflects renal involvement + intravascular hemolysis); (3) TACHYCARDIA; (4) HYPOTENSION (can progress to shock); (5) HEMOGLOBINURIA (red/dark/cola-colored urine from intravascular hemolysis + free hemoglobin in urine); (6) DYSPNEA + tachypnea; (7) CHEST PAIN; (8) NAUSEA + VOMITING; (9) SENSE OF IMPENDING DOOM; (10) FLUSHING + diaphoresis; (11) HEADACHE; (12) BLEEDING / DIC (later finding); (13) ANURIA / AKI (later); (14) SHOCK + multi-organ failure (severe); (15) DEATH possible. PATHOPHYSIOLOGY: recipient antibodies (anti-A + anti-B in O recipient + others depending on type) bind donor RBC ABO antigens → COMPLEMENT activation → INTRAVASCULAR HEMOLYSIS → release of free hemoglobin + cytokines (IL-1, IL-6, IL-8, TNF) + activation of coagulation cascade → DIC + AKI from heme pigment nephrotoxicity + tubular obstruction + shock. IMMEDIATE NURSING RESPONSE per AABB + institutional protocols: (1) STOP TRANSFUSION IMMEDIATELY at FIRST SIGN of reaction — clamp transfusion tubing within seconds of recognition (do NOT wait for severity); (2) MAINTAIN IV ACCESS with NS via SEPARATE TUBING — NOT same line — disconnect transfusion tubing entirely + connect new tubing + new bag of NS to existing IV access; rationale = continued infusion through same line continues blood exposure; NS prevents IV access loss + supports BP + UOP; (3) NOTIFY PROVIDER + BLOOD BANK STAT (phone or pager); (4) VITAL SIGNS q5-15 min initially (continuous monitoring with arterial line + cardiac monitor if hemodynamically unstable); (5) ASSESS for symptoms + hemoglobinuria + shock + DIC + bleeding; (6) MAINTAIN IV FLUIDS aggressive to support BP + URINE OUTPUT >100 mL/hr — heme pigment nephrotoxicity + tubular obstruction; LR or NS; some protocols use mannitol or alkaline diuresis (sodium bicarbonate to alkalinize urine pH >7) for heme-induced AKI prevention but this is provider-directed + variable; (7) POST-TRANSFUSION SAMPLE COLLECTION immediately for blood bank workup: BLOOD = repeat ABO/Rh + cross-match (compare to original) + DIRECT ANTIGLOBULIN TEST (DAT/Coombs — positive in AHTR) + FREE HEMOGLOBIN + LDH (elevated) + HAPTOGLOBIN (low/absent — consumed by free hemoglobin) + BILIRUBIN (indirect elevated) + DIC PANEL (PT + PTT + fibrinogen + D-dimer + platelets); URINE = hemoglobin + UA; visual inspection of plasma (pink/red = hemolysis); (8) RETURN remaining blood unit + tubing + filter + administration set to BLOOD BANK for analysis + investigation; (9) DOCUMENTATION meticulous + exact time of reaction + symptoms + interventions + responses + provider notification + blood bank workup + sentinel event reporting; (10) SUPPORT patient + family emotionally (acute fear + uncertainty); (11) PARTICIPATE in sentinel event root cause analysis + system improvement + just culture; (12) FAMILY NOTIFICATION + transparent communication per institutional policy + hospital risk management. ADDITIONAL INTERVENTIONS as needed: VASOPRESSORS (norepinephrine) for shock not responsive to fluids; ANTIHISTAMINES (diphenhydramine 50 mg IV) for allergic component; CORTICOSTEROIDS (methylprednisolone) considered; OXYGEN supplemental; INTUBATION if respiratory failure; BLOOD PRODUCTS (FFP, platelets, cryo) if DIC + bleeding (compatible blood after re-typing); DIALYSIS if AKI severe + uremia + acidosis + hyperkalemia. AHTR PROGNOSIS variable — mild cases recover with supportive care; severe cases (large volume incompatible + delayed recognition) can be FATAL or have permanent AKI + multi-organ failure. FATALITY rate from ABO-incompatible transfusion = significant in untreated severe cases; rapid recognition + STOP + supportive care dramatically improves outcomes.
labs.r: AHTR recognition + IMMEDIATE STOP + maintain IV access via SEPARATE tubing with NS + escalation + supportive care + samples + return unit + documentation + family + sentinel event = critical RN response. Each minute matters. Standard practice per AABB + institutional protocols. RN role: bedside recognition + rapid response + comprehensive care.

labs.v: DIFFERENTIAL of transfusion reactions important for management + appropriate intervention: (1) ACUTE HEMOLYTIC TRANSFUSION REACTION (AHTR) — minutes-1 hr; ABO incompatibility most common cause + usually CLERICAL ERROR; FEVER + CHILLS + BACK/FLANK PAIN + hypotension + HEMOGLOBINURIA + DIC + AKI risk; immediate STOP + supportive + samples + return unit + investigation; (2) FEBRILE NON-HEMOLYTIC TRANSFUSION REACTION (FNHTR) — most COMMON reaction; fever ≥1°C above baseline + chills WITHOUT hemolysis; cytokine release from donor white cells; LEUKOREDUCED blood reduces incidence; STOP transfusion to rule out AHTR (especially if first reaction or unable to distinguish); acetaminophen 650 mg PO; can sometimes restart slowly after symptoms resolve + AHTR ruled out; pretreatment with acetaminophen for known frequent reactions; (3) ALLERGIC REACTIONS — mild (urticaria + pruritus) to severe (anaphylaxis); minutes-hours; MILD = STOP + diphenhydramine 25-50 mg IV + may restart slowly if mild + symptoms resolved; SEVERE/ANAPHYLAXIS = STOP + EPINEPHRINE 0.3-0.5 mg IM thigh (1:1000 = 1 mg/mL) + IV fluids + airway + corticosteroids + diphenhydramine + supportive; classic in IgA-DEFICIENT patients with anti-IgA antibodies (rare but severe + need WASHED cells future); (4) TRANSFUSION-RELATED ACUTE LUNG INJURY (TRALI) — within 6 hr (typically 1-2 hr); HYPOXIA (SpO2 <90 on room air) + bilateral pulmonary edema NONCARDIOGENIC (similar to ARDS pattern) + fever + tachypnea + tachycardia; CXR bilateral pulmonary infiltrates; NORMAL or low CVP/wedge (vs TACO with elevated); donor antibodies (HLA + neutrophil antibodies from female multiparous donors) cause neutrophil sequestration + capillary leak in lungs; STOP + supportive + ventilatory support (mechanical ventilation often needed) + diuretics NOT helpful (noncardiogenic); MOST COMMON CAUSE OF TRANSFUSION-RELATED MORTALITY per FDA; prevention = male-only or never-pregnant donors for high-volume plasma products; (5) TRANSFUSION-ASSOCIATED CIRCULATORY OVERLOAD (TACO) — within 6 hr (typically during or shortly after); FLUID OVERLOAD + dyspnea + HTN + JVD + tachycardia + S3 + crackles + pulmonary edema CARDIOGENIC + bilateral infiltrates; CXR similar to TRALI; ELEVATED CVP/wedge (distinguishing from TRALI); common in elderly + heart failure + renal failure + rapid transfusion + volume sensitive; STOP + diuresis (furosemide IV) + slow rate + reassess + reduce volume + supplemental O2 + elevate HOB; preventable with slower rates + pre-medication with diuretic in volume-sensitive patients; (6) BACTERIAL CONTAMINATION (transfusion sepsis) — typically within hours (~30 min - 2 hr); fever + chills + HYPOTENSION + shock + rigors; PLATELETS at room temperature most often contaminated than RBC (refrigerated); STOP + blood cultures + GRAM STAIN of blood unit + broad-spectrum antibiotics + supportive + report to blood bank + culture remaining unit + tubing; severe cases life-threatening; prevention = aseptic collection + screening + storage protocols; (7) DELAYED HEMOLYTIC TRANSFUSION REACTION (DHTR) — 3-14 days post-transfusion; milder than AHTR + Hgb drop + jaundice + positive DAT + antibody screening reveals new antibodies; alloimmunization to non-ABO antigens (Rh, Kidd, Duffy, Kell common); supportive + identify antibodies + future transfusions with compatible blood; (8) GRAFT-VS-HOST DISEASE (TA-GVHD) — rare but FATAL; transfusion of immunocompetent donor T-cells to immunocompromised host (BMT + chemotherapy + congenital immunodeficiency + intrauterine + Hodgkin lymphoma); 4-30 days post-transfusion; fever + rash + diarrhea + pancytopenia + liver dysfunction; PREVENTION = IRRADIATED blood products in at-risk patients (mandatory); (9) IRON OVERLOAD — chronic transfusion patients (sickle cell + thalassemia + MDS); deferoxamine + deferasirox chelation; (10) IATROGENIC ELECTROLYTE = citrate hypocalcemia (after large transfusion — citrate chelates calcium; replace), hyperkalemia (from RBC release especially older units + large volumes), hypothermia (warm blood for massive transfusion). TRANSFUSION-TRANSMITTED INFECTIONS rare with current screening — HIV + HCV + HBV + HTLV + syphilis + WNV + Zika + Babesia + Chagas + screening + donor deferral; CMV via leukoreduction reduces risk. RISK STRATIFICATION = age + comorbidities + volume + rate + product type + indication + alternatives + informed consent + risk discussion.
labs.r: DIFFERENTIAL of transfusion reactions important for appropriate management. AHTR = STOP + supportive + samples + return unit + investigation; FNHTR = STOP + acetaminophen + may restart; ALLERGIC mild = STOP + diphenhydramine + may restart, severe = STOP + epi + supportive; TRALI = STOP + supportive + ventilatory + most common transfusion mortality; TACO = STOP + diuresis + slow rate; BACTERIAL = STOP + cultures + antibiotics + supportive; DHTR = identify antibodies + future compatible; GVHD = irradiated blood prevention. RN role: recognize + differentiate + appropriate response + escalate + document.

labs.n: SENTINEL EVENT + ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS + JUST CULTURE
labs.v: SENTINEL EVENT = unexpected occurrence involving DEATH or SERIOUS PHYSICAL/PSYCHOLOGICAL INJURY or RISK thereof (Joint Commission definition); ABO-incompatible transfusion = SENTINEL EVENT requiring institutional response + root cause analysis + reporting + system improvement; AHTR with serious injury (even without death) qualifies + warrants full sentinel event protocol. ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS (RCA) per Joint Commission: structured + thorough investigation of an event to identify underlying systemic + process factors that contributed; FRAMEWORK includes: (1) ESTABLISH the team — diverse stakeholders + leadership + frontline staff + risk management + ethics + quality + patient representative; (2) GATHER data — interviews + observations + chart review + system review + timeline; (3) IDENTIFY contributing factors — communication + personnel + equipment + environment + procedures + culture; (4) DEVELOP RECOMMENDATIONS — system changes + protocol revisions + education + technology + culture; (5) IMPLEMENT + monitor + evaluate effectiveness. FOR THIS CASE: contributing factors include — (a) bedside RN drawing cross-match sample did NOT perform 2-patient identifier check + used bed location label alone (proximal cause); (b) room transfer happened between sample collection + transfusion creating opportunity for confusion (contextual factor); (c) institutional protocol may not have explicit step-by-step procedures for sample collection + 2-identifier verification + bed location vs patient identifiers (system gap); (d) workload + distraction + interruptions (work environment factor); (e) lack of barcode scanning at sample collection (technology gap); (f) potentially inadequate education + training reinforcement (knowledge factor); (g) culture of efficiency + speed potentially competing with safety culture. JUST CULTURE = framework for analyzing errors that balances accountability + system thinking; distinguishes between: (1) HUMAN ERROR (inadvertent — slip + lapse + mistake; manage through processes + procedures + training + system improvements; NOT punitive); (2) AT-RISK BEHAVIOR (drift from procedures + risk perception underestimated; coach + identify why + reinforce safe behavior + manage through system + culture); (3) RECKLESS BEHAVIOR (conscious disregard of substantial + unjustifiable risk; appropriate to discipline + punitive); GOAL = encourage reporting + transparency + system learning + prevention rather than punishment for unintentional errors. RESPONSE for THIS CASE: (1) FAMILY NOTIFICATION + transparent communication of what happened + immediate apology + commitment to investigation + system improvement + ongoing care + emotional support + risk management involvement; (2) BEDSIDE RN involved in sample collection — JUST CULTURE assessment; this appears HUMAN ERROR + AT-RISK BEHAVIOR (drift from 2-identifier protocol); response = coaching + retraining + reinforcement + system improvements + not punitive (unless reckless pattern); RN may need emotional support (second victim phenomenon — staff trauma from error); EAP + peer support; (3) SYSTEM IMPROVEMENTS — barcode scanning at sample collection + bedside verification + 2-identifier reinforcement + protocol revision + education + audits + transfusion safety initiative; potentially technology investment + workflow redesign; (4) INSTITUTIONAL REPORTING — Joint Commission Sentinel Event database (voluntary) + state health department (mandatory in some states) + FDA MedWatch + AABB + internal patient safety incident reporting; (5) PATIENT SAFETY ORGANIZATION (PSO) protections for shared learning + protected from discovery; (6) LITIGATION risk + hospital legal + transparent communication + apology often reduces litigation; (7) MEDIA + reputation considerations per institutional policy; (8) ONGOING follow-up + monitoring + audit. SECOND VICTIM phenomenon — clinicians involved in medical errors experience emotional trauma + isolation + may have anxiety + depression + leave profession; institutional support + EAP + peer support + just culture + transparent investigation + system focus on improvement rather than blame critical. TEAM DEBRIEFING + emotional support + chaplain + EAP throughout.
labs.r: SENTINEL EVENT + ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS + JUST CULTURE framework for managing serious adverse events. RCA structured investigation. Just Culture balances accountability + system thinking. Second victim phenomenon. RN role: participate in investigation + transparent reporting + support colleagues + system improvement + lifelong learning.

labs.v: POST-AHTR monitoring + management for THIS PATIENT (typical 24-72 hr surveillance + ongoing): (1) CARDIOVASCULAR — HR + BP + arterial line + telemetry continuous initially; IV fluids titrated to MAP ≥65; vasopressors if needed (none required in this patient); arrhythmia monitoring (atrial fibrillation + ventricular arrhythmia possible with electrolytes + ischemia); cardiac enzymes if chest pain or ECG changes; (2) RESPIRATORY — SpO2 + supplemental O2; assess for ARDS from hemolysis + inflammatory response; CXR; ABG; intubation if respiratory failure (not needed in this patient); (3) RENAL — UOP hourly via Foley + maintain >100 mL/hr to flush heme pigments; BMP frequently (Cr trending); IV fluids generous (LR or NS) + furosemide if volume overload + retain heme pigments; ALKALINE DIURESIS controversial (sodium bicarbonate to alkalinize urine pH >7 to prevent heme pigment precipitation in tubules) — provider-directed + variable practice; MONITOR for AKI (Cr rising + UOP dropping + uremia + acidosis + hyperkalemia); DIALYSIS if severe; (4) DIC SURVEILLANCE — PT/PTT/fibrinogen/D-dimer/platelets q4-6h × first 24 hr then daily; TREAT DIC if bleeding/active or laboratory derangement — FFP + cryo + platelets as needed; aggressive support; (5) HEMOLYSIS labs — LDH + haptoglobin + bilirubin + free hemoglobin + DAT trending; (6) ELECTROLYTES — frequent monitoring; replace as needed; calcium hypocalcemia from citrate; hyperkalemia from RBC release; (7) PAIN management — appropriate analgesia for back/flank pain + post-op + procedural; (8) ANTIPYRETIC — acetaminophen for fever; (9) ANTIBIOTICS — consider if bacterial contamination suspected (vs AHTR); (10) BLOOD PRODUCTS — additional transfusion as needed AFTER reaction worked up + new sample drawn (24-72 hr later) + new cross-match + ensured ABO/Rh compatibility (use O negative emergency-release if urgent transfusion needed before workup complete); (11) SURGICAL site monitoring for bleeding + ischemia + complications; (12) EMOTIONAL support + family + chaplain + counseling; (13) FOLLOW-UP — antibody screen at later date (sensitization may develop or pre-existing antibodies identified) + documentation in chart + medical alert + patient education about transfusion history; (14) INVESTIGATION cooperation. PROGNOSIS for THIS PATIENT (recognized within 30 min + immediate stop + supportive care + no severe DIC + no AKI + supportive care + family supportive) = expected full recovery from AHTR; continued post-op recovery from AAA repair; psychological impact + family + patient education important; long-term blood product caution + medical alert + antibody screening + planning for future transfusions if needed.
labs.r: POST-AHTR monitoring + complication management = cardiovascular + respiratory + renal + DIC + electrolytes + emotional + investigation cooperation. AKI from heme pigment nephrotoxicity primary concern + maintain UOP >100 mL/hr + IV fluids generous. Standard ICU + post-op care. RN role: comprehensive monitoring + escalation + patient + family support + cooperation with investigation.

PROTECTIVE FACTORS: RAPID RECOGNITION by primary RN (within 30 min of reaction onset — essential for outcome); IMMEDIATE STOP of transfusion within 60 seconds of recognition (prevented additional incompatible blood exposure); MAINTAINED IV ACCESS via separate tubing with NS (continued fluid support + UOP support); RAPID ESCALATION to charge RN + intensivist + blood bank; AGGRESSIVE FLUID RESUSCITATION supporting hemodynamics + UOP; ALL APPROPRIATE labs + samples sent immediately; UNIT RETURNED to blood bank; EXTENSIVE MONITORING; INSTITUTIONAL SENTINEL EVENT response activated immediately + just culture + family communication; FAMILY supportive + appropriate + receptive to explanation + apology + commitment to investigation; PATIENT ALERT + cooperative + recovering. nursesNote: 1530 — Primary RN J. Wilson IDENTIFIED ACUTE TRANSFUSION REACTION in patient G. O'Brien (67 y/o male POD 1 after open AAA repair) during 2nd unit PRBC transfusion at 30 minutes into infusion. CONTEXT: POD 2 morning 0830 cross-match sample drawn for planned 2-unit PRBC for post-op Hgb 7.2 (down from 13.4 preop; significant operative blood loss + 2 intraop units already given). UNIT 1 transfused 1300-1500 UNEVENTFULLY with 2-RN verification + 2-patient identifier check + barcode scanning + slow start + vital signs + monitoring + post-transfusion completion + Hgb up to 8.2. UNIT 2 started 1500 with same protocols (2-RN verification at bedside, 2-patient identifier check using NAME + DOB at wristband, barcode scanning, slow start at 50 mL/hr, vital signs pre-transfusion stable HR 86 BP 124/76 RR 16 SpO2 97% T 37.3). AT 1530 (30 min into Unit 2) patient verbalized 'I'm so cold + my back is killing me + I feel terrible' + visible RIGORS + chills + diaphoresis + flushing + acute back/flank pain + nausea + vomiting × 2 + DARK AMBER/COLA-COLORED URINE visible in Foley collection bag; immediate vital signs: HR 126 (up from 86) + BP 94/56 (down from 124/76, MAP 69) + RR 26 + SpO2 95% on 2L NC + T 39.0°C (RAPID RISE from 37.3 over 30 min). PRIMARY RN RECOGNIZED ACUTE HEMOLYTIC TRANSFUSION REACTION (AHTR) given classic constellation = fever + chills + back/flank pain + tachycardia + hypotension + hemoglobinuria + timing within 30 min of starting blood + active transfusion. IMMEDIATE NURSING RESPONSE per AABB + institutional protocol: (1) STOPPED TRANSFUSION IMMEDIATELY at 1530:30 (clamped transfusion tubing within seconds of recognition; did NOT wait for severity); (2) MAINTAINED IV ACCESS via SEPARATE TUBING with NS — disconnected transfusion tubing entirely + opened new tubing + new infusion bag of NS + connected to existing IV access (NOT same line; continued blood exposure prevented; NS supported BP + UOP); (3) CALLED for HELP via charge RN bell + cardiac monitor alarm; charge RN C. Martinez arrived 1532; ICU intensivist Dr. Khan arrived 1535; (4) NOTIFIED PROVIDER + BLOOD BANK STAT by phone; (5) CONTINUOUS VITAL SIGNS monitoring (q5-15 min) with cardiac monitor; pulse oximetry continuous; (6) IV FLUID RESUSCITATION = 1L LR bolus + reassess + ongoing maintenance fluids; (7) URINE OUTPUT monitoring via Foley + target >100 mL/hr to flush heme pigments; (8) POST-TRANSFUSION LABS drawn immediately: BLOOD = repeat ABO/Rh + cross-match + DAT/Coombs + free hemoglobin + LDH + haptoglobin + bilirubin + DIC panel (PT + PTT + fibrinogen + D-dimer + platelets) + CBC + BMP + LFT + UA; URINE = hemoglobin + UA; (9) REMAINING UNIT 2 + tubing + filter + administration set returned to blood bank for analysis; (10) DOCUMENTATION meticulous + exact time of reaction onset + symptoms + interventions + responses + provider notification + blood bank workup; (11) PATIENT supportive care + acetaminophen 650 mg PO for fever + anti-emetic + reassurance + emotional support; (12) WIFE notified + at bedside + emotional support; (13) BLOOD BANK supervisor + hospital risk management + administration notified per sentinel event protocol; (14) TRANSFER TO ICU activated at 1535 for arterial line + central line + continuous monitoring + supportive care + intensivist management. INVESTIGATION (1700 results received from blood bank + chart review): post-transfusion DAT POSITIVE (recipient antibodies bound to donor RBCs) + free hemoglobin elevated + low haptoglobin + elevated LDH + indirect bilirubin elevated = confirms AHTR with intravascular hemolysis; CRITICAL FINDING — chart review revealed CROSS-MATCH MISMATCH: cross-match sample drawn this morning (0830) was actually drawn from the WRONG patient in the adjacent bed (a different patient who had moved into Bed 612 after G. O'Brien transferred to Bed 414 yesterday); bedside RN drawing the sample did NOT perform 2-patient identifier check + used bed location label alone for sample collection + labeled tube based on bed label assuming patient in Bed 414 was G. O'Brien (correct) but actually drew from adjacent bed (incorrect — different patient); the lab cross-matched against this WRONG SAMPLE + dispensed 2 units against this wrong sample's blood type; Unit 2 happened to be ABO INCOMPATIBLE with G. O'Brien (G. O'Brien is O+ which has anti-A + anti-B antibodies; donor unit was likely B+ or AB+); Unit 1 was the correctly cross-matched leftover from prior appropriate sample and was tolerated. SENTINEL EVENT confirmed + root cause analysis activated + hospital risk management + just culture review + family meeting at 0900 next morning. Patient stabilized to ICU 1545 with arterial line + central line + continuous monitoring + IV fluids + supportive care + intensivist management. 1700 ICU: HR 98, BP 118/68 MAP 85, RR 18, SpO2 97% on 4L NC, T 37.8 (acetaminophen given + improving); UOP 80 mL/hr; symptoms resolving (chills + rigors resolved + nausea improved + back pain improving + dark urine clearing); DIC panel negative; Cr 1.0 (no AKI emerging); recovery trajectory favorable. ALL LABORATORY AND SENTINEL EVENT documentation forwarded to blood bank + risk management + hospital quality. Family meeting Day 2 0900 with surgeon + intensivist + risk management + chaplain + transparent communication of error + apology + commitment to investigation + system improvement + ongoing care; patient + wife receptive + appreciative of explanation + grateful for rapid response + ongoing care; family supportive throughout. ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS scheduled within 72 hr per Joint Commission sentinel event protocol; involved RN coordinated with risk management + just culture assessment (appears HUMAN ERROR + AT-RISK BEHAVIOR — drift from 2-identifier protocol + system gap; not reckless; appropriate response = coaching + retraining + reinforcement + system improvements + not punitive); EAP + peer support offered to involved RN (second victim phenomenon); SYSTEM IMPROVEMENTS identified — barcode scanning at sample collection + bedside verification protocols enhanced + 2-identifier reinforcement training + protocol revision + audits + transfusion safety initiative + technology investment + workflow redesign + staff education; INSTITUTIONAL REPORTING to Joint Commission Sentinel Event database + AABB + state health department + FDA MedWatch + internal patient safety incident reporting + Patient Safety Organization for protected learning. Patient continued recovery — Day 2 morning 0700: HR 84, BP 126/76 MAP 93, RR 16, SpO2 97% on 2L NC, T 37.2; alert + oriented + recovered + back pain resolved + no further hypotension + UOP >50 mL/hr; UA cleared of hemoglobinuria; labs trending favorably; no further intervention needed; appropriate POD 2 trajectory + recovery from AHTR + ongoing recovery from AAA repair. PLAN: continue ICU monitoring × 24-48 hr post-AHTR; transition to floor when stable; cross-match new sample with strict 2-patient identifier verification before any future transfusion + use O negative emergency-release if urgent need; antibody screen at later date (sensitization may develop or pre-existing antibodies identified); document transfusion reaction in chart + medical alert bracelet recommended; patient + family education about transfusion history + future considerations; surgical recovery from AAA repair + standard post-op care + early mobility when stable + DVT prophylaxis + pain management + nutrition + multidisciplinary care; follow-up + ongoing involvement in sentinel event investigation + system improvement; emotional support throughout.

STOP TRANSFUSION immediately (done 1530:30)

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters at this AHTR recognition?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically + safety + sentinel event significant cues requiring nursing attention.

opts.text: ACUTE HEMOLYTIC TRANSFUSION REACTION (AHTR) at 30 min into 2nd unit PRBC: fever 39.0°C (UP from 37.3 — 1.7°C in 30 min) + chills + rigors + back/flank pain 7/10 + tachycardia HR 126 (up from 86) + hypotension BP 94/56 MAP 69 (down from 124/76) + dark amber/cola-colored urine in Foley + nausea/vomiting + diaphoresis + 'I feel terrible'; CLASSIC AHTR presentation
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — classic AHTR clinical features + timing within 30 min of starting blood + active transfusion. RECOGNITION is the most critical RN role. Foundation for IMMEDIATE response.

opts.text: IMMEDIATE NURSING RESPONSE: (1) STOP TRANSFUSION IMMEDIATELY at first sign (clamp tubing within seconds); (2) MAINTAIN IV ACCESS via SEPARATE TUBING with NS (disconnect transfusion tubing entirely + new tubing + new NS bag — NOT same line); (3) NOTIFY PROVIDER + BLOOD BANK STAT; (4) Continuous VS monitoring + cardiac + pulse ox; (5) IV fluid bolus + maintenance; (6) Acetaminophen for fever
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — immediate response framework. STOP + SEPARATE LINE + ESCALATE + supportive. Each minute matters. Standard practice. RN role: rapid bedside response.

opts.text: POST-TRANSFUSION SAMPLES collected immediately: BLOOD = repeat ABO/Rh + cross-match + DAT/Coombs + free hemoglobin + LDH + haptoglobin + bilirubin + DIC panel + CBC + BMP + LFT + UA + visual inspection of plasma; URINE = hemoglobin + UA; REMAINING UNIT + tubing + filter returned to blood bank for analysis + investigation
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — diagnostic confirmation + investigation. Standard post-transfusion reaction workup. RN role: sample collection + chain of custody + blood bank coordination.

opts.text: URINE OUTPUT MAINTENANCE >100 mL/hr to flush heme pigments + prevent AKI from heme pigment nephrotoxicity + tubular obstruction; IV fluids aggressive (LR or NS); alkaline diuresis consideration (sodium bicarbonate to alkalinize urine pH >7) provider-directed + variable; DIC + AKI + multi-organ failure surveillance
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — kidney protection from heme pigment nephrotoxicity. UOP >100 mL/hr + aggressive fluid + monitoring. AHTR-induced AKI common + serious. RN role: hourly UOP + fluid management + escalation.

opts.text: INVESTIGATION reveals CLERICAL ERROR — cross-match sample drawn this morning was actually from WRONG patient in adjacent bed; bedside RN did NOT perform 2-PATIENT IDENTIFIER CHECK at sample collection + used bed location label alone; SENTINEL EVENT confirmed; ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS activated within 72 hr per Joint Commission
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — sentinel event recognition + system root cause. Foundation for investigation + improvement. Just culture + transparent + just-not-punitive (human error + at-risk behavior; not reckless). RN role: cooperation + system improvement.

opts.text: TRANSFUSION SAFETY PROCEDURES per AABB + Joint Commission: 2-PATIENT IDENTIFIER CHECK (name + DOB or MRN) at EVERY step (sample collection + cross-match label + bedside + administration); 2-RN INDEPENDENT VERIFICATION at bedside (label + bag + patient identifiers + ABO/Rh + expiration + appearance + cross-match form + signatures); barcode scanning; slow start; vital signs; monitoring
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — transfusion safety framework. Multi-layered system. Wrong identification at ANY step = AHTR risk. RN role: 2-patient identifier + 2-RN verification + bedside monitoring.

opts.text: FAMILY TRANSPARENT COMMUNICATION + APOLOGY + commitment to investigation + system improvement + ongoing care + emotional support; risk management + hospital legal + chaplain + social worker; family meeting Day 2 with surgeon + intensivist + risk management + chaplain; just culture approach for involved RN (second victim phenomenon — emotional trauma + EAP + peer support)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — family-centered + institutional response. Transparency + apology often reduces litigation + builds trust. Just culture for staff. Foundation for ethical institutional response + learning.

opts.text: DIFFERENTIAL of transfusion reactions important — AHTR vs FNHR vs allergic mild/severe vs TRALI vs TACO vs bacterial sepsis vs delayed hemolytic vs GVHD vs iron overload vs electrolyte; each has different management; RECOGNITION + DIFFERENTIATION + APPROPRIATE response essential
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — differential. Each reaction type has specific management. RN role: recognize + differentiate + appropriate response + escalate.

opts.text: Allergies: iodine contrast (mild rash; not anaphylactic) + shellfish (mild GI upset) + NKDA medications; document and proceed; AHTR not related to allergies
opts.rat: Allergy documentation. AHTR is not allergic reaction (different pathophysiology — antibody to RBC antigens vs allergic). Document and proceed. Not the priority cue but important for safety.

opts.text: CONTINUE the transfusion of Unit 2 at slower rate + give acetaminophen + diphenhydramine to treat symptoms — appears to be a mild febrile non-hemolytic reaction (FNHR) which is the most common transfusion reaction and not life-threatening
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS approach. The constellation of FEVER + CHILLS + BACK/FLANK PAIN + HYPOTENSION + HEMOGLOBINURIA is CLASSIC AHTR (not FNHR which has fever + chills WITHOUT hemolysis + hypotension + back pain). STOP transfusion at first sign of ANY reaction to RULE OUT AHTR. Continuing transfusion of incompatible blood = life-threatening + can be FATAL. Standard practice: STOP first + investigate + differentiate + manage.

opts.text: REMOVE the IV line entirely + restart fresh IV access on the other arm before initiating any further interventions — the existing IV may be contaminated with blood + serve as a source of ongoing exposure
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG approach. The IV ACCESS itself is fine — what matters is REPLACING the transfusion TUBING with new tubing + new NS infusion via SEPARATE tubing (NOT same line). Removing IV + restarting wastes time + creates delay in supportive care + adds patient distress. Standard practice: disconnect transfusion tubing + connect new tubing + new NS to existing IV access + immediate supportive care.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What transfusion safety + reaction + sentinel event principles apply?
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying principles.

opts.text: AHTR PATHOPHYSIOLOGY: recipient antibodies (anti-A + anti-B in O recipient or others) bind donor RBC ABO antigens → COMPLEMENT activation → INTRAVASCULAR hemolysis → free hemoglobin release → cytokines + coagulation activation → DIC + AKI from heme pigment nephrotoxicity + tubular obstruction + shock; CLINICAL FEATURES = fever + chills + back/flank pain + hypotension + hemoglobinuria + DIC + AKI within minutes-1 hr
opts.rat: CORRECT — AHTR pathophysiology + clinical features. Foundation for recognition + management. Standard biomedical knowledge.

opts.text: IMMEDIATE RN RESPONSE: STOP transfusion + maintain IV access via SEPARATE TUBING with NS + notify provider + blood bank STAT + continuous VS + IV fluid bolus + UOP >100 mL/hr + post-transfusion samples + return remaining unit + supportive care + documentation + family + sentinel event
opts.rat: CORRECT — immediate RN response framework. STOP + SEPARATE LINE + ESCALATE + supportive + investigation. Standard practice per AABB + institutional protocols.

opts.text: DIFFERENTIAL OF TRANSFUSION REACTIONS: AHTR vs FNHR vs allergic mild/severe (anaphylaxis) vs TRALI (within 6 hr; bilateral pulmonary edema noncardiogenic + hypoxia; most common cause transfusion mortality per FDA) vs TACO (within 6 hr; cardiogenic pulmonary edema + JVD + HTN + tachycardia + elderly + HF) vs bacterial sepsis (fever + chills + hypotension + ~30 min-2 hr; platelets at room temp most often contaminated) vs delayed hemolytic (3-14 days; alloimmunization) vs GVHD (rare + fatal; irradiated blood prevention) vs iron overload + electrolyte (citrate hypocalcemia + hyperkalemia)
opts.rat: CORRECT — comprehensive differential. Each type has different management. RN role: recognize + differentiate + appropriate response.

opts.text: TRANSFUSION SAFETY per AABB + Joint Commission: 2-PATIENT IDENTIFIER CHECK (name + DOB or MRN) at EVERY step (sample collection + cross-match + bedside + administration); 2-RN INDEPENDENT VERIFICATION at bedside; barcode scanning; ABO compatibility table (O universal donor RBC + AB universal recipient RBC; AB universal donor plasma + O universal recipient plasma); leukoreduced + irradiated + washed products for at-risk patients; slow start + VS + monitoring + documentation
opts.rat: CORRECT — transfusion safety framework. Multi-layered. Wrong identification at any step = AHTR risk. Standard Joint Commission + AABB requirements.

opts.text: SENTINEL EVENT per Joint Commission = unexpected occurrence involving death or serious injury or risk thereof; ABO-incompatible transfusion qualifies; ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS within 72 hr; JUST CULTURE framework balances accountability + system thinking; distinguish HUMAN ERROR (process improvements + retraining + not punitive) vs AT-RISK BEHAVIOR (coaching + drift identification + system) vs RECKLESS BEHAVIOR (appropriate discipline); SECOND VICTIM phenomenon (staff trauma + EAP + peer support)
opts.rat: CORRECT — sentinel event + RCA + just culture framework. Patient safety culture. System learning. Foundation for institutional improvement + supporting involved staff.

opts.text: POST-AHTR MONITORING: cardiovascular (HR + BP + arterial line + telemetry + IV fluids + vasopressors PRN); respiratory (SpO2 + supplemental O2 + intubation if respiratory failure); renal (UOP >100 mL/hr + IV fluids generous + alkaline diuresis consideration + AKI surveillance + dialysis if severe); DIC surveillance + treatment if bleeding; electrolytes; pain + antipyretic + emotional support
opts.rat: CORRECT — comprehensive post-AHTR management framework. Multi-system + supportive care + monitoring + escalation. Standard ICU + post-op care.

The constellation = CLASSIC AHTR (fever + chills + BACK/FLANK PAIN + HYPOTENSION + HEMOGLOBINURIA — NOT FNHR which has fever + chills WITHOUT hypotension + hemolysis).
